content:
Hold-out evaluation predates modern ML. **Train/test** splits appear in classical **statistics** and **psychometrics** — fit a model on one sample, validate predictive accuracy on a fresh sample drawn from the same population.

Early computing-era pattern recognition (1960s–1970s) used simple train/test partitions because datasets were small and cross-validation was expensive to run by hand.

The **validation set** as a distinct third partition became standard in **neural network** research of the late 1980s and 1990s — practitioners needed a set for **hyperparameter tuning** and **early stopping** without burning the final test set on every experiment.

**Breiman** and others formalized **out-of-bag** and **cross-validation** estimates as alternatives when a single validation slice is too noisy — but the three-way hold-out pattern remained the default mental model in textbooks and industry.

**scikit-learn** (2010s) codified `train_test_split` and stratified options, making reproducible splits a one-liner in Python pipelines. **MLOps** practices now version split indices, enforce locked test sets in experiment trackers, and pair hold-out design with **group** and **temporal** split utilities for leakage-prone domains.

The core idea unchanged: **separate data used for learning from data used for choosing models from data used for final honest evaluation.**

timeline.label: Classical hold-out (1900s–1960s)
timeline.description: Statisticians evaluate predictive models on independent samples — early train/test thinking in regression and psychometrics.

timeline.label: Validation set emergence (1980s–1990s)
timeline.description: Neural net and ML practitioners add a validation partition for hyperparameter search and early stopping alongside a locked test set.

timeline.label: Cross-validation formalization (1990s)
timeline.description: k-fold CV supplements or replaces fixed validation when datasets are small — test set often remains for final check.

timeline.label: Library-standard splits (2010s)
timeline.description: scikit-learn and similar tools make stratified and reproducible train/val/test splits routine in Python workflows.

timeline.label: Leakage-aware splits (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Group, temporal, and nested CV patterns address domain-specific leakage — split design becomes a first-class MLOps concern.
